coredata.dc:title: Aptamer-mediated carbon dots as fluorescent signal for ultrasensitive detection of carbendazim in vegetables and fruits
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Yue
Yu, Hongyan
Niu, Xiaojuan
Wang, Qiaodie
Liu, Yuanyuan
Wu, Yuangen
coredata.dc:description: Pesticide residues like carbendazim have always been a great threat to agricultural production and food safety. Herein, a fluorescent aptasensor for carbendazim detection was established based on aptamer-mediated quenching of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) toward carbon dots (CDs). CZ-13 aptamer can wrap AuNPs and make them dispersed in salt solution, thus quench the fluorescence of CDs. On adding carbendazim, the aptamer is firstly consumed to preferentially combine with pesticides, resulting in the fluorescence recovery of CDs from quenching status. The degree of fluorescence recovery is positively correlated with pesticide concentration, which enables us to establish a fluorescence aptasensor for carbendazim detection. Under optimal conditions, the limit of detection of the fluorescent aptasensor is determined as low as 2.3 μg·L−1. Moreover, the average recovery range of carbendazim in several vegetables and fruits is from 86 % to 99 %, indicating that such aptasensor may have bright application prospects for carbendazim detection in foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pesticide
Rapid detection
Food analysis
Gold nanoparticles
Internal filtration effect
Biological recognition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of different types of CDs
Preparation of different types of AuNPs
Detection of carbendazim in food samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32160603 , 31760486 ), the Guizhou Provincial Science and Technology Foundation (ZK[2021]Key Project 037 ), the Guizhou Provincial Outstanding Young Science and Technology Talent Project ( [2021]5628 ), and the Open Foundation of Key Laboratory of Wuliangye-flavor Liquor Solid-state Fermentation ( 2021JJ015 ), China National Light Industry .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs. (B) Size distribution histogram of CDs. (C) Excitation, emission and UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs. Inset: CDs solution under visible light and UV lamplight. (D) Fluorescence (FL) emission spectra of CDs at various excitation wavelengths.

Visual fluorescence (A) and spectra images (B) of different solutions containing CDs. Sample 1: CDs; Sample 2: Sample 1 + AuNPs; Sample 3: Sample 2 + NaCl; Sample 4: Sample 3 + 200 μg·L−1 carbendazim; Sample 5: Sample 3 + Aptamer; Sample 6: 50 μg·L−1 carbendazim + Sample 5; Sample 7: 100 μg·L−1 carbendazim + Sample 5; Sample 8: 200 μg·L−1 carbendazim + Sample 5. The other conditions: 0.5 mg·L−1 CDs, 1 nM AuNPs, 20 mM NaCl and 20 nM Aptamer.

(A) Visual fluorescence of the sensing solutions. (B) Relationship between fluorescence variations and the concentration of carbendazim. Inset: fluorescence variations of sensing solutions respond to low concentrations of carbendazim (y = 0.578 x + 8.987, R2 =0.995).

(A) Visual fluorescence of the aptasensor treated with different competitive pesticides (Sample 1–6 successively represent carbendazim, profenofos, dimethoate, phorate, phoxim and paraquat). (B) Fluorescence variation responds to carbendazim and other competitive pesticides. The quantity of all pesticides was 200 μg·L−1.
Schematic diagram of the fluorescent assay platform for carbendazim detection.
Determination of carbendazim pesticide in real food samples.

head.ce:title: Aptamer-mediated carbon dots as fluorescent signal for ultrasensitive detection of carbendazim in vegetables and fruits

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University, Huaxi District

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pesticide residues like carbendazim have always been a great threat to agricultural production and food safety. Herein, a fluorescent aptasensor for carbendazim detection was established based on aptamer-mediated quenching of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) toward carbon dots (CDs). CZ-13 aptamer can wrap AuNPs and make them dispersed in salt solution, thus quench the fluorescence of CDs. On adding carbendazim, the aptamer is firstly consumed to preferentially combine with pesticides, resulting in the fluorescence recovery of CDs from quenching status. The degree of fluorescence recovery is positively correlated with pesticide concentration, which enables us to establish a fluorescence aptasensor for carbendazim detection. Under optimal conditions, the limit of detection of the fluorescent aptasensor is determined as low as 2.3 μg·L−1. Moreover, the average recovery range of carbendazim in several vegetables and fruits is from 86 % to 99 %, indicating that such aptasensor may have bright application prospects for carbendazim detection in foods.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CZ-13 aptamer sequences can wrap gold nanoparticles and make them dispersed in salt solution to quench the fluorescence of carbon dots, which facilitate us to establish a facile and ultrasensitive fluorescent aptasensor for the detection of carbendazim residue in foods.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbendazim is a broad-spectrum fungicide with high efficiency and low toxicity, which is widely utilized in the prophylaxis and treatment of fungal infections in China ((Xu et al., 2018). Researchers have found that carbendazim has reproductive toxicity to male mice and may be a potential human carcinogen (Akbarsha et al., 2001). Many countries and regions have implemented controls on carbendazim pesticide residues in agricultural products and food. In China, the maximum residue limit (MRL) of carbendazim in fruits and vegetables was set as 5 mg·kg−1 (Wang et al., 2020). Besides, the carbendazim pesticide is banned in most areas of the United States and the European Union (Ouyang et al., 2021). Traditional methods for carbendazim detection exhibit extraordinary performance include gas chromatography (Tzatzarakis et al., 2020), high-performance liquid chromatography (Li et al., 2020), etc. However, these methods are usually time-consuming, costly, and require delicate equipment as well as highly trained operators (Li et al., 2021; Zeng et al., 2021), which greatly limits their application in the detection of actual pesticide residues. The rapid methods for carbendazim detection like colorimetry are simple, visual and convenient, but the colorimetric results are easily affected by lots of environmental factors.

Recently, fluorescent methods have attracted much attention of researchers due to their characteristics of low background signal, high sensitivity, easy operation and rapid assay speed. Traditional fluorescent methods generally use organic dye molecules or quantum dot materials (Wegner and Hildebrandt, 2015) that have specific recognition for certain substances. The fluorescence emitted by most organic dye molecules is easy to decay. Quantum dots are metal-semiconductor nanocrystals with good light stability, but most of them contain heavy metals with biological toxicity, which limits their future applications (Pandey and Bodas, 2020). Therefore, it is urgent to find more stable and safer material as an alternative for fluorescent sources. Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of luminescent carbon nanomaterials discovered by Xu et al. (2004) during the purification of carbon nanotubes. Because of their unique luminescence performance, good biocompatibility, chemical stability, low toxicity and easy synthesis (Xu et al., 2019), CDs have become an ideal fluorescent probe in recent years and has been widely applied in bioimaging (Luo et al., 2013), immunoassay (Lin et al., 2017), small molecule detection (Batool et al., 2020), etc. Meanwhile, as a potential substitute for traditional fluorescent signal elements, CDs have been used to establish fluorescent sensors by combining AuNPs (Korram et al., 2019) and MnO2 nanowire (Dewangan et al., 2021) for rapid detection of organophosphorus pesticides. Recently, some fluorescent sensors based on CDs were developed for the rapid detection of carbendazim (Yang et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020). However, the above sensing systems lack specific recognition molecules, which makes them may face some challenges in practical applications.

Combining the excellent fluorescence characteristics of CDs and the specific recognition of aptamers, our group have established several fluorescent aptasensors for hazards analysis in foods (Wang et al., 2018), which have the advantages of low background signal, high specificity, low toxicity and good biocompatibility. It was found that the dispersed gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have good extinction ability, which can significantly quench the fluorescence signal of CDs through the internal filtration effect (IFE). Herein, a facile and sensitive fluorescent aptasensor was established for detecting carbendazim based on quenching the fluorescence of CDs by AuNPs. The whole detection principle is shown in Scheme 1. The CZ-13 aptamer can encapsulate and disperse AuNPs by electrostatic adsorption, which can effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs. When carbendazim is added, the CZ-13 sequences are firstly exhausted due to the preferential binding with pesticide, causing AuNPs to aggregate in a high concentration of salt. Therefore, the aggregated AuNPs no longer quench the subsequent fluorescence of CDs, leading the sensing solution to display strong blue fluorescence at 440 nm. The constructed fluorescence aptasensor is green, economical and easy to fabricate, which has good application prospects in the detection of carbendazim residues in foods.

CZ-13 aptamer was synthesized and purified by Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and its sequence is 5′-CGACACAGCGGAGGCCACCCGCCCACCAGCCCCTGCAGCTCCTGTA CCTGTGTGTGTG-3′). The aptamer sequence was denatured at 95 ℃ for 5 min, then cooled to room temperature before use. Tetrachloroauric acid (HAuCl4), sodium citrate, citric acid, ethylenediamine (EDA), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), carbodiimide (EDC), polydiallyl dimethyl ammonium chloride (PDDA), NaCl, formamide, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), carbendazim and other competitive pesticides like profenofos, dimethoate, phorate, phoxim, paraquat were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and nitric acid (HNO3) were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Unless otherwise stated, all chemical reagents were analytical grade and did not require other treatment and purification before use. The actual food samples like tomato, cucumber, and lemon were purchased from local supermarkets. The double-distilled water was used throughout the experiment. All glassware used in the experiment was thoroughly immersed with freshly prepared aqua regia solution (HCl:HNO3, 3:1).

The F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was utilized to record the fluorescence spectra with a 400 V voltage, excitation and emission slit of 10 nm. The UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was adopted to record the UV-Vis absorption spectra of various solutions. The morphology and size of nanoparticles were measured using the transmission electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). The aptamer sequences were denatured by an oscillating thermostatic metal bath (Yiheng, Shanghai).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of different types of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: According to the previous reports, three kinds of CDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method. The CDs-1 was prepared with citric acid and EDA (Wang et al., 2018). Briefly, 1.26 g citric acid and 1608 μL EDA was dissolved in 30 mL water. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor and heated at 180 ℃ for 5 h. The obtained CDs-1 was purified by a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 300 Da and stored at 4 ℃ before use. The CDs-2 was prepared with citric acid and phosphoric acid (Parvin and Mandal, 2017). In Brief, 1.5 g citric acid was dissolved in 10 mL water. Subsequently, 10 mL H3PO4, 1 mL EDA and 0.01 mg EDC were added and mixed thoroughly. Then, the resulting solution was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor and heated at 250 ℃ for 2 h. The reaction solution was centrifuged at 11,200 rpm for 10 min, and the obtained precipitate was dissolved in ethanol and centrifuged, which was repeated three times. Finally, the precipitate was dispersed in water after being filtered by a microporous membrane with 0.22 µm and stored at 4 ℃ for subsequent use. Referred to the previous report (Chen et al., 2017), CDs-3 was prepared using citric acid and formamide. The 0.6 g citric acid and 2 mL formamide was dissolved in 18 mL water and then transferred to a hydrothermal reactor and heated at 190 ℃ for 3 h. The obtained CDs-3 was purified by dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 300 Da and stored at 4 ℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of different types of AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Three kinds of AuNPs with different modifications were prepared to refer the previous reports. For the AuNPs modified with citric acid (CC-AuNPs) (Wang et al., 2018), 10 mL of sodium citrate solution (38.8 mM) was added to 100 mL of boiling HAuCl4 (1 mM) solution. After heating and stirring for 15 min, the above solution was stirred for 2 h at room temperature to obtain stable and dispersed AuNPs solution. For unmodified AuNPs (Um-AuNPs) (Deng et al., 2014), 3 mL of NaBH4 solution (264.34 mM) was firstly added to 120 mL of HAuCl4 (0.37 mM). After stirring for 30 min, the mixed solution was stabilized in the dark for 12 h to obtain unmodified AuNPs. In the case of AuNPs modified with PDDA (PDDA-AuNPs) (Das et al., 2016), 0.25 mL of PDDA solution (4 %, W/W) was dissolved in 40 mL water, and then 0.2 mL NaOH solution (0.5 M) as well as 0.1 mL HAuCl4 solution (29 mM) were added. The obtained mixture was heated to 100 °C and kept for several minutes. Finally, the excess polymer in the solution was removed by centrifugation to obtain the stable dispersion of PDDA-AuNPs.

For the fluorescent aptasensor, 5 μL CZ-13 aptamer and 100 μL of AuNPs (1 nM) was thoroughly mixed in a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube and incubated at 30 °C for 10 min. Then, 40 μL NaCl (20 mM) solution was added into the mixed solution and incubated at 30 °C for another 10 min. Finally, 5 μL CDs (0.5 mg·L−1), 5 μL carbendazim with different concentrations and 345 μL water were added to the centrifuge tube to form a 500 μL system. After reacting at 30 ℃ for 30 min, the above solution was added into a quartz cuvette for subsequent fluorescence measurement. For the blank solution, the pesticide was replaced by the same volume of water. The fluorescence intensities of the test sample (F) and the blank sample (F0) were measured, and the fluorescence variation (F-F0) was chosen as the sensing signal to determine the response of the established aptasensor for carbendazim.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of carbendazim in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: Several common vegetables and fruits such as tomato, cucumber and lemon were chosen to validate the applicability of the proposed aptasensor for carbendazim in food analysis. The pretreatment of food samples refers to the reported literature (Huang et al., 2017). Firstly, the real samples were washed and dried thoroughly. The carbendazim with known concentration was evenly sprayed on the surface of the vegetable and fruit samples, which were naturally dried for 24 h to fully absorb the pesticide. Then, the dried samples were cut into squares about 1 cm2 in size and extracted with 20 % methanol for 30 min. Finally, the extract was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane to obtain the final sample treatment solution for further determination.

The surface morphologies of the CDs were investigated by TEM ( Fig. 1A), which confirmed that CDs exhibits a uniform spherical structure with good dispersibility. Further analysis displays that the average size of CDs is about 6 nm (Fig. 1B), which is similar with the previous reports. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained to investigate the optical properties of CDs, and the results were shown in Fig. 1C. The aqueous solution of CDs is observed to be light yellow under sunlight. At the same time, it emits strong blue fluorescence under UV lamp, which proves that the prepared CDs possesses excellent fluorescence property. Besides, the UV–vis spectrum shows that CDs has a strong absorption peak at 345 nm, which may be related to the n-π * (CO) transition (Barman and Patra, 2018). The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs were 360 nm and 440 nm, and the Stokes shift was relatively large (80 nm), indicating that CDs has high fluorescence emission efficiency and excellent anti-interference capacity (Yan et al., 2019). The result in Fig. 1D exhibits that the maximum emission wavelength of CDs does not change with the movement of excitation wavelength. This phenomenon proves that the fluorescence emission wavelength is not dependent on excitation light, and its luminescence mechanism is surface defects rather than size dependence bandgap transition (Barman and Patra, 2018). According to our previous research (Wang et al., 2018), the surface groups (e.g. -NH2, -OH and -COOH) endows CDs with good fluorescence and hydrophilic properties (Zhao and Zhu, 2018).

To validate the feasibility of the as-prepared fluorescent aptasensor for the detection of carbendazim, the fluorescence spectra of various solutions were recorded. As displayed in Fig. 2, the CDs can emit bright blue fluorescence alone, and their fluorescence spectrum has a strong characteristic peak at 440 nm (Sample 1). When a certain quantity of AuNPs is added, the fluorescence of CDs is significantly quenched by AuNPs (Sample 2). Interestingly, CDs could emit strong blue fluorescence in Sample 2 after adding a high concentration of NaCl (Sample 3). This result can be attributed to that AuNPs are aggregated by the large quantity of salt and no longer quench the fluorescence of CDs. When carbendazim was added to CDs solution, the fluorescence did not change significantly (Sample 4), proving that carbendazim itself would not interfere with the fluorescence of the CDs. Once CZ-13 aptamer sequences were added into Sample 3 to dispersed AuNPs, the fluorescence of CDs is significantly quenched. After adding carbendazim into Sample 5, the fluorescence of the sensing solution gradually recovered (Sample 6–8), and the fluorescence variation was positively correlated to the concentration of pesticide. The above results demonstrate that the addition of CZ-13 aptamer enables AuNPs to effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs, while carbendazim pesticide can exhaust aptamer sequences and further restore the fluorescence.

According to previous report (Zu et al., 2017), the IFE of quencher on CDs has the following remarkable properties. (1) The absorption spectrum of the quencher overlaps the excitation or emission spectrum of the CDs. (2) The absorption peak of CDs does not change in the presence of quencher. In the subsequent experiments, the fluorescence spectra of CDs and the absorption spectra of AuNPs were compared. The data in Fig. S1 exhibit that the absorption spectrum of dispersed AuNPs can overlap with the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs. It was displayed that even if CDs and AuNPs were mixed together, their absorption peaks did not change (Fig. S2). Therefore, the quenching effect of AuNPs toward CDs can be confirmed as the IFE. It is worth noting that the results in Fig. S1 also confirmed that the absorption spectrum of the aggregated AuNPs only slightly overlapped with the emission spectrum of CDs, which may be the reason why the aggregated AuNPs could not quench CDs.

To further investigate the interaction among AuNPs, NaCl and aptamers, the spectra and corresponding color of solutions containing different substances are recorded in Fig. S3. The AuNPs alone appear distinctly red color and exhibit a strong characteristic absorption peak at 520 nm (Sample 1). When a certain amount of NaCl was added to the AuNPs solution, the AuNPs rapidly aggregated and its color changed from red to black (Sample 2). In this case, the Sample 2 exhibits a strong absorption peak at 680 nm, while the absorption peak at 520 nm is almost negligible. Surprisingly, the aptamer can maintain the dispersed state of AuNPs under high quantity of NaCl (Sample 3), and the characteristic peak of AuNPs still appears at 520 nm. The above results demonstrate that AuNPs can aggregate in high concentration of NaCl, while aptamers can maintain the disperse status of AuNPs even in higher content of salt. To further determine the interaction between aptamer and target, carbendazim was added to Sample 3, then the AuNPs aggregated and the absorption peak appeared around 680 nm (Sample 4). The results clearly prove that the aptamer can specifically bind to carbendazim but not AuNPs.

To improve the sensitivity of the fluorescent aptasensor for carbendazim detection, numerous parameters including AuNPs, CDs, NaCl and aptamer concentration, were investigated. According to the UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs and the independent fluorescence properties of CDs, 360 nm was employed as the excitation wavelength of CDs.

Considering that the interaction between AuNPs and aptamer may be related to surface charge, the optical properties of CC-AuNPs with negative charges, PDDA-AuNPs with positive charges and Um-AuNPs with strong negative charges were investigated. As shown in Fig. S4A, the absorption peaks of the three AuNPs are at 400–550 nm, which may enable them to possess the ability to quench the fluorescence of CDs by IFE. The influence of AuNPs with different charges on the fluorescent aptasensor was further investigated in Fig. S4B. The CC-AuNPs and PDDA-AuNPs can both obviously quench the fluorescence of CDs, but the quenching effect of Um-AuNPs is not obvious. The reason is that the CC-AuNPs can effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs through IFE. The positively charged PDDA-AuNPs may have an irreversible static quenching effect with the negatively charged CDs (Barman and Patra, 2018), thereby quenching the fluorescence of CDs. Moreover, there is electrostatic repulsion between the strongly negatively charged Um-AuNPs and CDs, so the quenching effect of Um-AuNPs on fluorescence is relatively weak (Yan et al., 2019). When the three AuNPs interacted with the aptamer under a high concentration of NaCl, the fluorescence of CDs was still quenched by AuNPs. The reason for this phenomenon is that the aptamer encapsulates the AuNPs, which increases the electronegativity of nanoparticles to prevent them from being aggregated by a high concentration of salt. However, when carbendazim was added, only the fluorescence of the sensing solution containing CC-AuNPs enhanced significantly. Therefore, CC-AuNPs was selected as the quencher of CDs, and their concentration was further optimized, as shown in Fig. S4C. As CC-AuNPs increased, the sensing signal gradually enhanced and mounted to a maximum when CC-AuNPs concentration is 1 nM. The reason for this phenomenon is that a low concentration of CC-AuNPs cannot completely quench the fluorescence. However, when the quantity of CC-AuNPs is too high, NaCl is not enough to make it aggregate completely. Therefore, CC-AuNPs with a concentration of 1 nM were selected to quench the fluorescence of CDs in the subsequent experiments.

As the fluorescence source of the aptasensor, CDs play a significant role in sensing solutions. Usually, the fluorescence property of CDs is related to their size and surface properties. Reference to previous reports (Chen et al., 2017; Parvin and Mandal, 2017; Wang et al., 2018), three CDs with different particle sizes and fluorescence yields were prepared. As exhibited in Fig. S5, the influence of three CDs on the sensing signal of the fluorescent aptasensor was investigated. When AuNPs were added, the fluorescence of the three CDs were all quenched significantly. This may be because AuNPs quenched the fluorescence of CDs-1 and CDs-3 through IFE and quenched the fluorescence of CDs-2 through irreversible quenching. When the CZ-13 aptamer was added into the above solution, the fluorescence of the three CDs did not change significantly. However, when carbendazim was added to the sensing solution, only the fluorescence of CDs-1 recovered obviously. Therefore, CDs-1 was selected as the signal element for the carbendazim fluorescent aptasensor.

The NaCl can regulate the dispersion state of AuNPs and further affect the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Herein, the effect of NaCl concentration on the aptasensor has been examined in Fig. S6. As NaCl increased, the sensing signal enhanced and mounted to a maximum when NaCl concentration is 20 mM. Therefore, 20 mM of NaCl was sufficient to construct the aptasensor for carbendazim detection. When the concentration of NaCl further increased, the sensing signal declined significantly. This is because that excessive NaCl makes the added aptamers unable to maintain the dispersed state of AuNPs, resulting in a high fluorescence value of the blank sample.

The aptamer can affect the dispersion of AuNPs in high concentrations of NaCl by adjusting the charge density on the surface of nanoparticles. Therefore, the effect of aptamer concentration on the fluorescent aptasensor was studied. As shown in Fig. S7, the sensing signal is positively correlated with the aptamer concentration and tends to be stable when the quantity is greater than 20 nM. When the content of aptamers is relatively low, the AuNPs aggregated in high concentration NaCl. On the contrary, if the concentration of aptamers is too high, the excessive part cannot bind to carbendazim, which can stabilize AuNPs to further quench the fluorescence of CDs.

Under optimal conditions, various amounts of carbendazim were added into the fluorescent aptasensor, and the sensing signal (F-F0) were recorded in Fig. 3. It was observed that the fluorescence under the UV lamp of the sensing solution gradually enhanced with the increased amount of pesticide (Fig. 3A), and the corresponding sensing signal also increased linearly (Fig. 3B). Once the quantity of carbendazim exceeds 400 μg·L−1, the sensing signal of the fluorescent aptasensor hardly increases, which means that the combination of CZ-13 aptamer and pesticide is saturated. Encouragingly, the change of fluorescence intensity can be easily discerned by the naked eyes once carbendazim exceeds 100 μg·L−1, which indicates that the proposed aptasensor can be conveniently applied to qualitative detection of carbendazim. In addition, the established aptasensor exhibits an excellent linear range of 7.7–200 μg·L−1. Calculated by 3σ/slope (Luo et al., 2021; Tang et al., 2021), the limit of detection (LOD) for carbendazim were calculated as low as 2.3 μg·L−1. Moreover, the limit of quantification (LOQ) was determined (S/N = 10) to be 7.7 μg·L−1, which is far lower than the permissible level in fruits and vegetables both set by the European Union and China. Therefore, the constructed fluorescent aptasensor with high sensitivity can accurately analyst amount of carbendazim.

Furthermore, the performance of the fluorescent aptasensor was compared with other methods for carbendazim detection (Table S1). The electrochemical detection shows excellent performance, but it often has cumbersome electrode processing technology and complex instruments (Zou et al., 2021). The colorimetric methods are simple, visual and convenient, but the colorimetric results are easily affected by lots of environmental factors. Therefore, the colorimetric methods still face many challenges in the practical application of carbendazim detection. The existing fluorescence methods usually use the organic dye Rhodamine B or FAM as the sensing element (Su et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020), which has certain physiological toxicity and the fluorescence signal is easily attenuated. Herein, the environmentally friendly CDs are chosen as the sensing material of the constructed fluorescence aptasensor. Accordingly, the established detection method is economical, environmentally friendly, and highly sensitive, which has bright application prospects in the analysis and detection of food samples.

To study the selectivity of the proposed fluorescent aptasensor, competitive pesticides like profenofos, dimethoate, phorate, phoxim, paraquat were individually added to the aptasensor. The corresponding fluorescent intensities of each sample are exhibited in Fig. 4A. It is noteworthy that only carbendazim can make the sensing solution emit a bright blue fluorescence. The results in Fig. 4B shows that the solution added with carbendazim displays strong fluorescence, and its sensing signal is significantly higher than that of the other solutions containing the competitive pesticides. Notably, the sensing solution containing carbendazim has almost the same absorbance variation regardless of whether or not competitive pesticides were added. The evidence proves that competitive pesticides have almost no interference on the proposed aptasensor for carbendazim detection.

Considering the residues of the pesticide on the surface of vegetables and fruits, the quantities of carbendazim in actual samples like tomato, cucumber and lemon were analyzed. The results in Table 1 show that the average recovery for carbendazim is in the range of 86 %−99 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is from 2.3 % to 9.4 %. The above results indicate that the proposed fluorescent aptasensor has good practicability and can be used as an alternative method for carbendazim analysis in vegetables and fruits.

In summary, a fluorescent aptasensor for carbendazim detection was established based on aptamer recognition and the quenching effect of AuNPs toward CDs fluorescence. The aptasensor exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity with a limit of detection as low as 2.3 μg·L−1 for carbendazim. The fluorescent aptasensor was used to detect carbendazim pesticides in several vegetables and fruits, and the recovery rate was 86 %−99 %. In short, the fluorescence aptasensor has the following significant advantages. (1) As the signal source of the fluorescent aptasensor, CDs are easy to prepare, economical, non-toxic and environmentally friendly. (2) The constructed aptasensor has the advantages of simple manufacture and low cost without surface modification of nanomaterials and labeling of aptamer sequences. (3) Due to the efficient IFE of AuNPs toward CDs and the specific binding of aptamer to carbendazim, the constructed fluorescent aptasensor exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity for carbendazim detection. Although the proposed fluorescence strategy has the above merits, it still has certain challenges in realizing remote and on-site detection. In the future, it will be a trend to develop test strips, hydrogels and other products for the fluorescence detection of carbendazim based on the sensing principle of this aptamer, and even build a smartphone detection platform.

Yue Tang: Data curation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Hongyan Yu: Investigation, Validation. Xiaojuan Niu: Investigation, Validation. Qiaodie Wang: Investigation, Validation. Yuanyuan Liu: Formal analysis, Supervision. Yuangen Wu: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32160603, 31760486), the Guizhou Provincial Science and Technology Foundation (ZK[2021]Key Project 037), the Guizhou Provincial Outstanding Young Science and Technology Talent Project ([2021]5628), and the Open Foundation of Key Laboratory of Wuliangye-flavor Liquor Solid-state Fermentation (2021JJ015), China National Light Industry.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2022.104730.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Table S1
Supplementary material
.
